items.q: Did Hitler seize power in a violent coup in 1933?
items.correct: No - he was legally appointed Chancellor in January 1933 through a deal brokered with President Hindenburg, then dismantled democracy from inside using the Reichstag Fire Decree and Enabling Act.
items.misconception: Hitler took power by leading a successful armed revolution in 1933.
items.random_wrong: Hitler became Chancellor by winning a national referendum.
items.why: Students project the failed 1923 Putsch onto 1933; the exam rewards explaining the legal, backstairs route to power.

items.q: Did the Nazis ever win a majority in a free election?
items.correct: No - their best result in a fully free election was about 37 per cent in July 1932; they never won an overall majority while elections remained free.
items.misconception: Most Germans voted the Nazis into power with a clear majority.
items.random_wrong: The Nazis won every German election after 1920.
items.why: Because Hitler ended up in total control, students assume overwhelming electoral support; in fact elite deals closed the gap the votes did not.

items.q: Did the 1923 hyperinflation directly bring Hitler to power?
items.correct: No - the Stresemann era restored stability after 1924, and it was the Depression from 1929 that revived Nazi fortunes; Hitler only became Chancellor in 1933.
items.misconception: Hyperinflation in 1923 led immediately to Hitler taking over Germany.
items.random_wrong: Hyperinflation was caused by Hitler to destroy the Weimar Republic.
items.why: Students compress the timeline; a decade and a second, different economic crisis separate the wheelbarrows of 1923 from the Chancellorship of 1933.

items.q: Which law allowed Hitler to make laws without the Reichstag?
items.why: Correct answer: The Enabling Act of 1933. “The Treaty of Versailles” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the immediate context of the 1923 hyperinflation crisis?
items.correct: The government printed money during the French occupation of the Ruhr
items.misconception: The Wall Street Crash destroyed German banks
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The government printed money during the French occupation of the Ruhr. “The Wall Street Crash destroyed German banks” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who were the main victims of the Night of the Long Knives in 1934?
items.correct: Ernst Röhm and the SA leadership, along with other rivals of Hitler
items.misconception: The leaders of the Communist Party only
items.why: Correct answer: Ernst Röhm and the SA leadership, along with other rivals of Hitler. “The leaders of the Communist Party only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: When did Hitler become Chancellor of Germany?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: January 1933. “November 1923” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Hitler become Chancellor?
items.why: Exact answer: 1933 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
